title: Can I wet sand wood?
problem: Wet sanding bare wood can raise the grain and make the surface feel fuzzy.
likely_cause: Water can swell wood fibers, especially on unfinished wood. Dry sanding is safer for normal wood prep.
recommended_grit: Use dry sanding for bare wood: 120, 180, then 220. Use wet sanding only on cured finishes when the finish maker allows it.
wet_or_dry: Dry for bare wood; wet only on cured finish coats.
steps: Dry sand bare wood with 120, 180, then 220.
Remove dust between grits.
Apply finish only after the surface feels even.
Wet sand only cured finish layers, not raw wood.
avoid: Do not soak bare wood while sanding.
success_check: The bare wood feels smooth and dry with no raised grain before finishing.
symptom: wood gets fuzzy after wet sanding
quick_answer: For bare wood, sand dry. Use 120, 180, then 220. Wet sanding is for cured finishes, not raw wood.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not wet sand raw wood before stain or paint.
Do not apply finish while the wood is damp.
search_phrases: wood gets fuzzy after wet sanding
should wood be sanded wet or dry
sandpaper for wood wet or dry

title: What grit before polyurethane?
problem: Wood needs a smooth but not over-polished surface before polyurethane.
likely_cause: Very coarse scratches can show through finish, while overly fine sanding can reduce finish grip on some woods.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220 before applying polyurethane. Between cured coats, use 320 or 400 lightly.
wet_or_dry: Dry before the first coat; dry or very light wet sanding between cured coats only if the finish instructions allow it.
steps: Sand bare wood through 180 and 220.
Remove all dust before applying polyurethane.
After a coat cures, scuff lightly with 320 or 400.
Clean dust before the next coat.
avoid: Do not use 1000+ grit on bare wood before polyurethane.
success_check: The wood is smooth, dust-free, and still has enough tooth for finish.
quick_answer: Before polyurethane, use 180 then 220 on bare wood. Between cured coats, scuff lightly with 320 or 400.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip dust removal before polyurethane.
Do not sand through edges between coats.
search_phrases: best grit for polyurethane on wood

title: 2000 grit does not make the surface shiny
problem: The surface looks smooth after 2000 grit but still looks dull or hazy.
likely_cause: Very fine sandpaper refines scratches but usually leaves a uniform sanding haze. Shine normally comes from polishing after sanding.
recommended_grit: Use 1500, 2000, then 3000 before polishing. After 3000, use the correct polish for the material.
wet_or_dry: Wet for clear coat, plastic, resin, and similar fine finishing work.
steps: Wet sand through 1500, 2000, and 3000.
Keep the surface clean and evenly wet.
Stop when the haze is uniform.
Polish after sanding to restore shine.
avoid: Do not expect 2000 grit alone to create a glossy finish.
success_check: The surface has an even fine haze with no deep scratches before polishing.
symptom: 2000 grit leaves haze instead of shine
quick_answer: 2000 grit refines the surface but does not create shine by itself. Use 3000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip polishing after fine wet sanding.
Do not chase shine by sanding one spot too long.

title: What grit for headlight restoration?
problem: Plastic headlights can stay cloudy or scratched after sanding.
likely_cause: Headlight lenses need a gradual wet sanding sequence before polishing and UV protection.
recommended_grit: Use 800 or 1000 for oxidation, then 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Wash the headlight lens.
Wet sand evenly with 800 or 1000 for oxidation.
Refine with 1500, 2000, then 3000.
Polish and protect the lens after sanding.
avoid: Do not stop at 800 or 1000 grit because the lens will stay hazy.
success_check: The lens has an even fine haze before polishing, with no deep sanding lines.
quick_answer: For headlights, wet sand 800 or 1000, then 1500, 2000, and 3000. Polish and protect after sanding.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not dry sand plastic headlights aggressively.
Do not skip final polish and protection.

title: What grit for acrylic plastic?
problem: Acrylic plastic can scratch, haze, or melt during sanding.
likely_cause: Acrylic needs light pressure and fine grit progression because coarse scratches can be difficult to remove.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for shaping, then 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine stages.
steps: Clean the acrylic surface.
Use light pressure and a flat backing where possible.
Polish after sanding if clarity is needed.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure that heats or gouges acrylic.
success_check: The acrylic has an even fine haze before polishing.
quick_answer: For acrylic, use light pressure. Start around 400 or 600, then wet sand through 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start too coarse on clear acrylic.
Do not let plastic dust or slurry build up.

title: Plastic melts or smears while sanding
problem: Plastic gets gummy, smeared, or soft during sanding.
likely_cause: Too much pressure, too much speed, or a clogged sheet can create heat and soften plastic.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 for prep, then 600 and finer with light pressure.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine stages to control heat and residue.
steps: Stop sanding and let the plastic cool.
Clean the paper and surface.
Resume with lighter pressure.
Use 600 or finer for final smoothing.
avoid: Do not keep sanding hot or gummy plastic.
success_check: The plastic cuts cleanly without smearing or softening.
quick_answer: Use light pressure, clean the sheet, and move to finer grits. For plastic, 320 or 400, then 600+ works best.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not press hard on plastic.
Do not keep using clogged paper on plastic.
search_phrases: how to sand plastic without melting

title: What grit for stainless steel appliance scratches?
problem: Stainless steel appliance scratches can look worse if the sanding direction is wrong.
likely_cause: Visible stainless surfaces often have a directional grain. Sanding across the grain creates mismatched lines.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for light scratch blending, then 800 or finer only for finer matching.
wet_or_dry: Dry first; wet can be used at finer stages with clean water.
steps: Find the direction of the stainless grain.
Test in a hidden spot first.
Sand lightly with the grain using 400 or 600.
Refine with 800 if the scratch pattern needs to be finer.
avoid: Do not sand across the visible stainless grain.
success_check: The repaired area follows the original grain direction and does not show cross-scratches.
quick_answer: For stainless appliance scratches, sand with the grain. Start with 400 or 600, then refine with 800.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use circular sanding on brushed stainless steel.
Do not start with coarse grit on a visible appliance surface.
search_phrases: what grit for stainless steel appliance scratches

title: What grit removes oxidation from aluminum?
problem: Aluminum has dull oxidation that does not come off with cleaning alone.
likely_cause: Oxidation needs a grit that cuts the dull surface layer before finer grits refine the marks.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 for oxidation, then 600, 800, and 1000 for finer prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for initial oxidation removal; wet for finer stages.
steps: Clean the aluminum first.
Sand oxidation with 320 or 400.
Refine with 600, then 800 or 1000.
Clean residue before finishing or polishing.
avoid: Do not jump from oxidation removal directly to polishing.
success_check: The dull oxidation is removed and the scratch pattern is even.
quick_answer: For aluminum oxidation, start with 320 or 400, then refine with 600, 800, and 1000.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use a loaded sheet on aluminum.
Do not leave aluminum dust on the surface between grits.
search_phrases: what grit removes oxidation from aluminum

title: What grit for cabinets before painting?
problem: Cabinet paint can peel or show scratches when the surface is not prepared correctly.
likely_cause: Gloss, grease, and old finish need to be cleaned and scuffed so primer or paint can bond.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 to scuff existing finish. Use 320 lightly between primer or paint coats.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Clean grease and residue first.
Scuff the cabinet surface with 180 or 220.
Remove dust completely.
Use 320 lightly between cured coats if needed.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy cabinet finish without scuff sanding.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed before primer or paint.
quick_answer: For cabinets before painting, clean first, then scuff with 180 or 220. Use 320 lightly between cured coats.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand dirty cabinet surfaces.
Do not use very coarse grit on cabinet faces unless stripping damage.
search_phrases: what grit for cabinets before painting

title: What grit for furniture before painting?
problem: Paint on furniture can show old scratches or fail to grip glossy finish.
likely_cause: Furniture needs a clean, dull, evenly scuffed surface before primer or paint.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for paint prep. Use 120 only for rough areas or old thick finish.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Clean the furniture surface.
Use 120 only on rough or damaged areas.
Scuff the full surface with 180 or 220.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not paint over dust, wax, oil, or glossy finish.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed.
quick_answer: For furniture before painting, use 180 or 220 after cleaning. Use 120 only on rough areas.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit across detailed furniture faces without a reason.
Do not skip cleaning before sanding.
search_phrases: what grit for furniture before painting
best sandpaper grit for painted furniture

title: What grit to feather paint edges?
problem: Paint repair edges remain visible after sanding.
likely_cause: The edge between old paint and bare or repaired areas was not blended gradually enough.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 to level the edge, then 400 or 600 for finer primer prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet can be used at 600 for fine finishing where suitable.
steps: Clean the repair area.
Sand the paint edge with 220 or 320.
Blend outward with light pressure.
Finish with 400 or 600 before primer.
avoid: Do not leave a hard paint ridge before primer.
success_check: The repair edge feels smooth and cannot be felt with a fingertip.
quick_answer: Feather paint edges with 220 or 320, then refine with 400 or 600 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not dig into one narrow edge line.
Do not skip primer prep after feathering paint.
search_phrases: what grit to feather paint edges

title: What grit before spray paint?
problem: Spray paint can show scratches, dust, or poor adhesion after sanding.
likely_cause: Spray paint needs a clean, dull surface with scratches fine enough to be covered by primer or paint.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 for general spray paint prep. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for most prep; wet for fine finishing stages when the coating instructions allow it.
steps: Clean the surface.
Sand with 320 or 400 for primer or paint prep.
Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
Remove all dust before spraying.
avoid: Do not spray paint over dust or glossy unsanded surface.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and evenly sanded before spraying.
quick_answer: For spray paint prep, use 320 or 400. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 80 or 120 grit right before spray paint.
Do not skip cleaning after sanding.

title: What grit sequence for epoxy resin polishing?
problem: Epoxy resin remains cloudy or scratched after sanding.
likely_cause: Resin needs a gradual wet sanding sequence before polish can restore clarity or gloss.
recommended_grit: Use 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, then 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Make sure the resin is fully cured.
Wet sand with 600 or 800 to level defects.
Refine through 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000.
Polish after the final sanding step.
avoid: Do not polish over deep 600-grit scratches.
success_check: The resin has a uniform fine haze with no visible deep lines before polishing.
quick_answer: For epoxy resin, wet sand 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand uncured resin.
Do not skip from 600 directly to polish.

title: What grit for fiberglass?
problem: Fiberglass repair or gelcoat prep needs sanding without leaving deep scratches.
likely_cause: Fiberglass work often needs a grit that levels repair material first, then finer grits for coating or polishing.
recommended_grit: Use 120 or 180 for shaping, 220 or 320 for prep, and 600+ for fine wet sanding.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet for fine finishing.
steps: Clean the fiberglass area.
Use 120 or 180 for rough shaping only.
Use 220 or 320 before primer or coating.
Use 600 and finer for wet finishing.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit as the final prep before paint or gelcoat finish.
success_check: The repair is level and the final scratch pattern matches the next coating step.
quick_answer: For fiberglass, shape with 120 or 180, prep with 220 or 320, and wet finish with 600+.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not breathe sanding dust; use proper dust control and protection.
Do not skip cleaning before coating.

title: What grit for wet sanding gelcoat?
problem: Gelcoat can remain dull, orange-peeled, or scratched after sanding.
likely_cause: Gelcoat needs fine wet sanding steps before polishing. Starting too coarse can create deep scratches.
recommended_grit: Use 800 or 1000 for leveling, then 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Wash the gelcoat surface.
Wet sand with 800 or 1000 for leveling.
Refine with 1500, 2000, and 3000.
Polish after sanding to restore gloss.
avoid: Do not stop at 800 or 1000 grit before polishing.
success_check: The gelcoat has an even fine haze before polish.
quick_answer: For gelcoat, wet sand 800 or 1000, then 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand dry when doing fine gelcoat finishing.
Do not use dirty water during wet sanding.
search_phrases: what grit for wet sanding gelcoat

title: Do I need a sanding block?
problem: Hand sanding leaves waves, finger marks, or uneven pressure marks.
likely_cause: Fingers apply pressure in narrow lines. A sanding block spreads pressure more evenly on flat surfaces.
recommended_grit: Use the same grit sequence, but wrap the sheet around a flat block for flat areas.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the sanding stage.
steps: Use a sanding block on flat surfaces.
Keep pressure light and even.
Change direction slightly between grits to see progress.
Use fingers only for curves and details.
avoid: Do not press with fingertips on flat panels.
success_check: The surface looks flat and has no finger-pressure grooves.
quick_answer: Use a sanding block on flat areas. It spreads pressure evenly and helps prevent waves and finger marks.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use fingertips as the main pressure point on flat surfaces.
Do not use a hard block on delicate curves.
search_phrases: do I need a sanding block
how to hand sand flat surface

title: How to sand corners by hand
problem: Corners and tight spaces are hard to sand evenly with a full sheet.
likely_cause: Loose paper can wrinkle or round over edges if it is not folded or supported.
recommended_grit: Use the same grit as the surrounding step, folded cleanly or wrapped around a small flat edge.
wet_or_dry: Dry for most corner sanding; wet only for fine finishing stages.
steps: Cut or tear a manageable piece of sandpaper.
Fold it into a firm edge or wrap it around a small block.
Sand with light pressure into the corner.
Match the same grit sequence used on the surrounding area.
avoid: Do not dig the folded edge into one spot.
success_check: The corner is smooth and matches the surrounding scratch pattern.
quick_answer: Fold or cut the sheet into a small piece and use the same grit sequence as the surrounding surface.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not round over sharp edges by pressing too hard.
Do not use a dirty folded edge on final prep.
search_phrases: how to sand corners by hand

title: Can I reuse wet sandpaper?
problem: Wet sandpaper may cut poorly after it has already been used.
likely_cause: Used wet sandpaper can hold slurry, loose grit, or worn abrasive that scratches or cuts unevenly.
recommended_grit: Reuse only while the abrasive still feels sharp and clean. Replace it when it feels smooth, loaded, or torn.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Rinse the sheet during use.
Inspect the abrasive surface before reusing it.
Reuse only if it still cuts evenly.
Switch to a fresh sheet for final finishing.
avoid: Do not reuse dirty or worn wet sandpaper on final finish steps.
success_check: The reused sheet cuts evenly and does not drag debris across the surface.
quick_answer: Reuse wet sandpaper only while it is clean and still cuts. Use a fresh sheet for final finishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not store used wet sheets dirty.
Do not reuse torn paper on a visible finish.

title: How to store sandpaper sheets
problem: Sandpaper sheets curl, bend, or feel uneven before use.
likely_cause: Moisture, heat, and poor storage can deform sheets or affect backing flatness.
recommended_grit: Store sheets flat, dry, and sealed when possible.
wet_or_dry: Storage guidance applies before both wet and dry use.
steps: Keep unused sheets flat.
Store them in a dry place.
Keep grits separated so coarse grit does not contaminate fine sheets.
Reseal packaging after opening.
avoid: Do not store sandpaper in damp areas or under heavy uneven weight.
success_check: The sheet stays flat, clean, and easy to use.
quick_answer: Store sandpaper flat, dry, sealed, and separated by grit. Keep coarse grit away from fine sheets.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not mix used coarse sheets with fine finishing sheets.
Do not store wet used sheets against clean new sheets.

title: Can I use sandpaper on concrete?
problem: Small concrete spots or edges need smoothing by hand.
likely_cause: Concrete is hard and abrasive, so hand sandpaper is best for small touch-ups, not heavy grinding.
recommended_grit: Use 60, 80, or 120 for small concrete touch-ups. Use finer grits only after the high spots are removed.
wet_or_dry: Dry; use dust control and protection.
steps: Clean loose debris.
Use 60 or 80 for rough high spots.
Move to 120 for smoother touch-up.
Stop when the edge or spot is level enough for the task.
avoid: Do not expect hand sandpaper to replace a grinder for large concrete areas.
success_check: The small concrete spot is smoother and no longer has sharp high points.
quick_answer: For small concrete touch-ups, use 60 or 80, then 120. Hand sheets are not for large concrete grinding.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use fine finishing grits first on rough concrete.
Do not sand concrete dust without protection.
search_phrases: can I use sandpaper on concrete
what grit for concrete touch up

title: Can sandpaper sharpen a knife?
problem: Knife edges can be refined with sandpaper, but the angle must stay controlled.
likely_cause: Sandpaper can abrade steel, but sharpening needs a stable backing and consistent angle.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for edge work, then 1000, 2000, or 3000 for finer refinement.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on backing and method.
steps: Attach the paper to a flat backing.
Hold a consistent sharpening angle.
Start with 400 or 600 only if the edge needs work.
Refine with 1000, 2000, or 3000.
avoid: Do not freehand on loose paper without angle control.
success_check: The edge is refined evenly with no rolled or uneven bevel.
quick_answer: Sandpaper can refine a knife edge when fixed to a flat backing. Use 400 or 600, then 1000, 2000, or 3000.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use torn or loose paper near a sharp edge.
Do not change the sharpening angle between strokes.

title: What grit for lawn mower blade touch-up?
problem: A lawn mower blade has burrs, rust, or rough edges after sharpening.
likely_cause: The blade needs coarse enough grit for burrs and rust, then medium grit for cleanup.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for rust and burr cleanup, then 180 or 220 for smoother edge cleanup.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Secure the blade safely before sanding.
Use 80 or 120 for burrs or rust.
Refine with 180 or 220.
Clean the blade before reinstalling.
avoid: Do not sand an unsecured blade.
success_check: The blade edge is cleaner with burrs reduced and loose rust removed.
quick_answer: For mower blade cleanup, use 80 or 120 for burrs and rust, then 180 or 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use fine polishing grit first on burrs.
Do not ignore blade balance after major sharpening work.
search_phrases: what grit for lawn mower blade
remove burrs from mower blade grit

title: What grit removes adhesive residue?
problem: Sticker, glue, or adhesive residue stays on the surface after cleaning.
likely_cause: Soft residue can smear and clog sandpaper if it is not removed carefully first.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 after scraping/cleaning residue. Use 320 for final prep before paint.
wet_or_dry: Dry for residue removal; wet only after residue is gone and the surface allows it.
steps: Remove as much adhesive as possible by scraping or cleaning.
Sand remaining residue with 180 or 220.
Replace the sheet when it loads up.
Finish with 320 before paint if needed.
avoid: Do not grind soft adhesive into the surface with heavy pressure.
success_check: The residue is gone and the surface has an even prep scratch pattern.
quick_answer: Remove most adhesive first, then sand with 180 or 220. Finish with 320 before paint.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not keep using loaded paper on adhesive.
Do not start with fine grit on thick residue.

title: What grit for PLA 3D prints?
problem: PLA 3D prints show layer lines after sanding.
likely_cause: Layer lines need gradual leveling. Too much pressure can heat or smear plastic.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for visible layer lines, then 400, 600, and 800 for smoother prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet for finer stages.
steps: Remove loose print defects first.
Use 220 or 320 for visible layer lines.
Refine with 400, 600, and 800.
Use light pressure to avoid heat buildup.
avoid: Do not press hard enough to soften the plastic.
success_check: The print feels smoother and layer lines are reduced evenly.
quick_answer: For PLA prints, use 220 or 320 for layer lines, then 400, 600, and 800 with light pressure.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start with very coarse grit on small print details.
Do not sand hot or gummy plastic.

title: What grit for ABS 3D prints?
problem: ABS 3D prints have layer lines or rough support marks.
likely_cause: ABS can be sanded, but coarse grit can damage detail and pressure can create heat.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for support marks, then 400, 600, and 800 for smoothing.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet for finer stages.
steps: Clean the print surface.
Sand support marks with 220 or 320.
Refine with 400, then 600 or 800.
Use light pressure around small details.
avoid: Do not over-sand small edges or printed details.
success_check: The print has reduced layer lines and an even scratch pattern.
quick_answer: For ABS prints, use 220 or 320 for marks, then 400, 600, and 800 for smoothing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use heavy pressure that rounds off details.
Do not skip cleaning between grits.

title: What grit removes rust before painting metal?
problem: Rust remains on metal before primer or paint.
likely_cause: Loose rust needs removal first, then the metal needs a finer scratch pattern for coating prep.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for rust removal, then 180, 220, and 320 before primer.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Remove loose rust and debris.
Sand rust with 80 or 120.
Refine with 180 and 220.
Finish with 320 before primer if a smoother prep is needed.
avoid: Do not paint over loose rust dust.
success_check: The rust is removed or stabilized as required and the surface is evenly prepared for primer.
quick_answer: For rust before painting, use 80 or 120, then refine with 180, 220, and 320 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not stop at coarse rust-removal scratches before paint.
Do not leave dust or loose rust under primer.
search_phrases: what grit removes rust before painting metal

title: What grit for cast iron rust?
problem: Cast iron has rust spots or rough oxidation.
likely_cause: Rust removal needs a grit that cuts oxidation without over-polishing the surface too early.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for rust, then 180 or 220 for smoother cleanup.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Remove loose rust first.
Sand rust spots with 80 or 120.
Refine with 180 or 220.
Clean the surface thoroughly after sanding.
avoid: Do not use ultra-fine grit first on active rust.
success_check: The rust spots are reduced and the surface feels more even.
quick_answer: For cast iron rust, use 80 or 120 first, then 180 or 220 for cleanup.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave rust dust on the surface.
Do not use a loaded sheet for final cleanup.
search_phrases: what grit for cast iron rust
remove rust from cast iron grit

title: What grit for clear coat dust nibs?
problem: Small dust nibs or raised specks are visible in clear coat.
likely_cause: Dust nibs need very fine leveling so the surrounding clear coat is not cut too aggressively.
recommended_grit: Use 1500 or 2000 for nib leveling, then 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Wet.
steps: Let the clear coat cure as directed.
Wet sand the nib lightly with 1500 or 2000.
Blend the small area with 3000.
Polish after the haze is even.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit on small clear coat dust nibs.
success_check: The nib is level and the area has a small even haze before polishing.
quick_answer: For clear coat dust nibs, wet sand lightly with 1500 or 2000, then 3000 before polishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand through edges or high spots.
Do not stay on the nib longer than needed.

title: How to sand small parts by hand
problem: Small parts are hard to hold and can get rounded or uneven while sanding.
likely_cause: Small pieces need controlled backing and light pressure so edges and details are not damaged.
recommended_grit: Use the grit required by the material, then step finer gradually.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on material and finish stage.
steps: Secure the small part safely.
Use a small block, stick, or folded sheet for control.
Sand lightly with the correct grit for the material.
Step finer only after scratches are even.
avoid: Do not hold small parts in a way that puts fingers near sharp edges or moving tools.
success_check: The part is evenly sanded without rounded details or gouged edges.
quick_answer: Secure the part, use a small backing block or folded sheet, and keep pressure light.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand small details with heavy pressure.
Do not skip grits on visible small parts.
search_phrases: how to sand small parts by hand

title: Does sandpaper expire?
problem: Old sandpaper may feel weak, curled, or less effective.
likely_cause: Sandpaper does not expire like food, but moisture, heat, dust, and bent storage can reduce performance.
recommended_grit: Use the same grit if the sheet is clean, flat, and still cuts. Replace old sheets that are curled, contaminated, or worn.
wet_or_dry: Applies to both wet and dry use.
steps: Inspect the old sheet before use.
Check if the abrasive still feels sharp.
Discard sheets that are dirty, curled, torn, or contaminated.
Use a fresh sheet for final finishing.
avoid: Do not use contaminated old sandpaper on a visible finish.
success_check: The sheet lies flat, cuts evenly, and does not shed debris.
quick_answer: Sandpaper does not have a simple expiration date. Use it only when it is clean, flat, and still cuts.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use old coarse grit near fine finishing sheets.
Do not use curled sheets for flat final prep.

title: What does sandpaper grit number mean?
problem: The grit number can be confusing when choosing sandpaper.
likely_cause: Lower grit numbers are coarser and remove more material. Higher numbers are finer and leave smaller scratches.
recommended_grit: Use low grits for removal, medium grits for prep, and high grits for fine finishing.
wet_or_dry: Applies to wet and dry sanding.
steps: Choose 60 to 120 for heavy removal.
Use 150 to 240 for general prep.
Use 280 to 600 for fine prep.
Use 800 to 3000 for wet sanding and polishing prep.
avoid: Do not choose the highest grit first when the surface still needs leveling.
success_check: The chosen grit matches the job: removal, prep, fine prep, or finishing.
quick_answer: Lower grit is coarser. Higher grit is finer. Use coarse for removal, medium for prep, and ultra-fine for finishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use one grit for every stage.
Do not skip from very coarse directly to ultra-fine.
search_phrases: what does sandpaper grit number mean

title: Silicon carbide vs aluminum oxide sandpaper
problem: Customers may not know when silicon carbide sandpaper is the right choice.
likely_cause: Silicon carbide is commonly used for wet/dry sanding and works well on hard materials, plastics, finishes, metal prep, and fine sanding.
recommended_grit: Use the grit sequence based on the surface, not only the abrasive material.
wet_or_dry: Wet or dry.
steps: Choose the grit by task first.
Use coarse grits for removal or shaping.
Use medium grits for prep.
Use fine and ultra-fine grits for wet sanding and finishing.
avoid: Do not choose sandpaper by abrasive material only and ignore grit progression.
success_check: The grit and abrasive match the material and finish goal.
quick_answer: Silicon carbide wet/dry sandpaper is useful for plastic, metal prep, finishes, and fine wet sanding. Choose grit by the task.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip grit progression just because the abrasive is sharp.
Do not use ultra-fine grit for heavy removal.
search_phrases: silicon carbide vs aluminum oxide sandpaper
what is silicon carbide sandpaper good for

title: Can wet/dry sandpaper be used without water?
problem: Wet/dry sandpaper can confuse customers because the name includes wet and dry.
likely_cause: Wet/dry sandpaper is designed for both methods, but the better method depends on the surface and finish stage.
recommended_grit: Use dry sanding for early shaping and wood prep. Use wet sanding for fine finishing, clear coat, plastic, resin, and polishing prep.
wet_or_dry: Wet or dry.
steps: Use dry sanding for removal, shaping, and bare wood prep.
Use wet sanding for fine finishing stages.
Keep wet sanding clean with fresh water.
Match the method to the surface and finish goal.
avoid: Do not wet sand bare wood as a default method.
success_check: The sanding method matches the surface and the final finish goal.
quick_answer: Yes. Wet/dry sandpaper can be used dry. Use wet sanding mainly for fine finishing and polishing prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not assume every job should be wet sanded.
Do not dry sand fine clear coat finishing unless the coating instructions allow it.
search_phrases: can wet dry sandpaper be used without water
do I need water with wet dry sandpaper

title: Sanding scratches show through paint
problem: Paint dries and reveals sanding scratches underneath.
likely_cause: The final prep grit was too coarse, scratches were not refined, or primer did not fill the sanding marks.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 before primer or paint. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for normal prep.
steps: Let the paint or primer cure before correcting.
Sand the scratches with 320 or 400.
Refine with 600 for finer topcoat prep.
Clean dust before repainting.
avoid: Do not repaint over visible coarse scratches.
success_check: The surface is smooth and scratches are not visible before the next coat.
quick_answer: Visible scratches under paint usually need 320 or 400 correction, then 600 for finer topcoat prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 120 or 180 as final paint prep on visible surfaces.
Do not skip primer where scratches need filling.
search_phrases: what grit to remove scratches before paint

title: What grit for wood end grain?
problem: Wood end grain can absorb more finish and look darker or rougher than face grain.
likely_cause: End grain fibers are exposed differently and often need careful refinement.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220. Use 320 lightly on end grain when a smoother finish is needed.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Sand the face grain through the normal sequence.
Sand end grain with 180 and 220.
Use 320 lightly on end grain for smoother absorption control.
Remove dust before finishing.
avoid: Do not leave end grain rough when the rest of the wood is smooth.
success_check: The end grain feels smooth and closer to the surrounding surface.
quick_answer: For wood end grain, use 180 then 220. Use 320 lightly when the end grain needs smoother finish control.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand one end grain spot and round the edge.
Do not skip dust removal before stain or finish.
search_phrases: what grit for wood end grain
wood end grain rough after sanding

title: What grit between polyurethane coats?
problem: Polyurethane coats can feel rough or show small dust nibs between coats.
likely_cause: Light scuff sanding helps level dust nibs and gives the next coat a clean surface.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 between cured polyurethane coats.
wet_or_dry: Dry; wet only when the finish instructions allow it.
steps: Let the polyurethane cure as directed.
Scuff lightly with 320 or 400.
Avoid sanding through edges.
Remove dust completely before the next coat.
avoid: Do not sand uncured polyurethane.
success_check: The coat feels smooth and dull without sanding through the finish.
quick_answer: Between cured polyurethane coats, scuff lightly with 320 or 400 and remove all dust.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit between polyurethane coats.
Do not apply the next coat over sanding dust.

title: What grit sequence for tabletop refinishing?
problem: A tabletop can show scratches, old finish marks, or uneven stain after sanding.
likely_cause: Large flat furniture surfaces need gradual grit steps and even pressure to avoid waves and visible scratches.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for old finish or roughness, then 180 and 220 before stain or finish.
wet_or_dry: Dry.
steps: Use a sanding block or flat backing.
Start with 120 only if old finish or roughness remains.
Sand evenly with 180, then 220.
Remove dust before stain or finish.
avoid: Do not use fingertips as the main pressure on a flat tabletop.
success_check: The tabletop feels flat and shows an even scratch pattern before finishing.
quick_answer: For tabletop refinishing, use 120 only for old finish or roughness, then 180 and 220 before finishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip from 120 directly to finish.
Do not sand across the grain on visible wood.
search_phrases: what grit sequence for tabletop refinishing
